items.text: A tramp, a gentleman, a poet, a dreamer, a lonely fellow, always hopeful of romance and adventure... He would have you believe he is a scientist, a musician, a duke, and a polo-player. However, he is not above picking up cigarette-butts or robbing a baby of its candy.
items.context: From My Autobiography (1964), where Chaplin describes the character of the Tramp he created, explaining the contradictions and complexity he built into his famous character.
items.publicReaction: The autobiography provided valuable insight into Chaplin's creative process and character development, becoming a significant work of film history and autobiography.

items.text: My prodigious sin was, and still is, being a non-conformist. Although I am not a Communist I refused to fall in line by hating them. Secondly, I was opposed to the Committee on Un-American Activities — a dishonest phrase to begin with, elastic enough to wrap around the throat and strangle the voice of any American citizen whose honest opinion is a minority of one.
items.context: From My Autobiography (1964), where Chaplin reflects on his persecution during the McCarthy era and his refusal to conform to anti-communist hysteria.
items.publicReaction: The statement was a powerful indictment of McCarthyism and vindicated many of Chaplin's positions. It resonated with readers as a historical commentary on American intolerance.

items.text: I always like walking in the rain, so no one can see me crying.
items.context: Reflective statement from My Autobiography (1964) regarding how Chaplin handled emotional pain privately despite his public persona as a comedian.
items.publicReaction: The quote became one of Chaplin's most widely cited statements, appreciated for its poignant contradiction between his public comedic role and private emotional depths.

items.text: I am what I am: an individual, unique and different, with a lineal history of ancestral promptings and urgings, a history of dreams, desires, and of special experiences, of all of which I am the sum total.
items.context: Philosophical statement from My Autobiography (1964) where Chaplin reflects on his identity and the complex forces that shaped him.
items.publicReaction: The passage was cited by scholars and readers as an important statement of Chaplin's philosophical worldview and sense of individuality.
